input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Twin delivers a suite of services: (1) real‑time monitoring of lighting and environmental parameters; (2) high‑fidelity simulation of energy usage and illumination scenarios; (3) optimisation of lighting schedules and set‑points to minimise energy consumption; (4) predictive analytics that forecast equipment degradation and occupancy‑driven lighting demand; (5) closed‑loop control that actuates dimmers and switches based on simulation outcomes; and (6) immersive 3D visualisation of the building state. Primary users are Facility Managers and building operators who receive dashboards, alerts, and interactive visual scenes to support decision‑making.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The framework provides real‑time data exchange that keeps the physical building and its digital counterpart continuously synchronized. Synchronization is performed continuously (i.e., as soon as sensor updates are available) and supports near‑zero latency required for live monitoring and control. The temporal granularity is tied to the frequency of incoming building‑automation data streams, and runtime configuration management allows these frequencies to be adjusted without redeploying the twin.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The DT framework acts as a single central hub that hosts the operational digital twin for a building. The architecture is presented as a centralized model in which enriched BIM models are instantiated as one digital‑twin instance per building, coordinated by the framework. No multiple‑instance or hierarchical deployment is described, so the current solution assumes a single‑instance, centralized deployment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a modular, service‑oriented architecture with standardized extension points that enable integration of custom services and analytics. Runtime configuration management permits on‑the‑fly adaptation of services. The DT Engine orchestrates bidirectional data exchange between enriched BIM models and the operational twin, orchestrating the flow of data through defined roles (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer) and acting as the central coordination component.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability is addressed by non‑functional requirements targeting heterogeneous systems. The framework exposes standardized interfaces for bidirectional data exchange, allowing BIM tools, building‑automation devices and external analytics services to connect via a plug‑in pattern using the defined extension points. Specific data exchange protocols are not enumerated, but the architecture is designed for integration with external systems through these standardized interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework builds on BIM as the geometric backbone and enriches it with device definitions (sensors, actuators, controllers). These enriched BIM models constitute the computational models: geometric models from BIM, behavioral models expressed as control‑logic scripts attached to the devices, and analytical models generated downstream for analytics. Data sources include the BIM repository (static design data) and live streams from the physical sensors. A model‑driven engineering (MDE) pipeline extracts structured representations from the enriched BIM and automatically generates deployable DT artifacts, establishing explicit links between each device instance and its BIM element.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators are modeled as BIM objects by the Building Control Engineer, establishing a semantic link to the physical device they represent. The digital twin runtime can issue remote commands to these actuators via the bidirectional, real‑time data‑exchange channel, enabling on‑demand control of lighting, HVAC, or other building services. The framework does not prescribe specific actuator types, actuation ranges, or safety constraints; it only guarantees that any defined actuator can be addressed through the standardized extension points and that actuation commands are synchronized continuously with the physical infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are also represented as BIM entities, allowing the Building Control Engineer to embed measurement points directly into the BIM model. These sensors capture post‑occupancy data and indoor environmental conditions and stream their readings continuously to the DT, enabling real‑time synchronization between the physical infrastructure and its digital counterpart. The collection mechanism leverages the same real‑time data exchange channel; specific sampling rates, accuracy or precision figures are not provided in the source text.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework provides real‑time data exchange that continuously synchronizes physical building sensors and actuators with their digital representations. Physical measurements are streamed from the building to the Digital Twin as they are produced, enabling the twin to maintain an up‑to‑date state. The document describes continuous synchronization but does not specify concrete data types, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event triggers, quality‑assurance steps, preprocessing, or integration mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Bidirectional communication is supported by the DT Framework, which acts as a central hub. Facility Managers and DT Engineers can issue control signals from the Digital Twin to physical devices for real‑time monitoring and control. The document mentions runtime interfaces and control signals but provides no detail on command types, message formats, validation, authentication, feedback loops, conflict resolution, or execution confirmation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT Framework is described as having a modular architecture with standardized extension points and a central hub that mediates bidirectional data exchange between physical assets and the digital twin. Non‑functional requirements such as interoperability and scalability are highlighted, yet the document does not disclose specific network protocols, topology, bandwidth or latency targets, redundancy mechanisms, or security protocols for the underlying communication infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework defines a four‑stage lifecycle for a building Digital Twin: (1) Device Modeling – Building Control Engineers enrich BIM models with explicit sensor, actuator and controller definitions, creating a unified semantic base; (2) Model‑Driven Transformation – Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) tools extract structured representations from the enriched BIM and automatically generate deployable DT artefacts; (3) Deployment & Activation – The generated artefacts are instantiated in the runtime environment, establishing real‑time data exchange links that keep the physical devices synchronised with their digital counterparts; (4) Operational Use & Evolution – Facility Managers interact with the Twin via immersive visualisation for monitoring and control, while the modular architecture’s standardized extension points allow DT Engineers to integrate custom services and analytics, supporting ongoing evolution of the Twin. Transition between stages is managed automatically by the MDE pipeline, and governance is implicit in the role‑based responsibilities (Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer) defined in the model‑driven deployment framework.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The DT is engineered through a Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) workflow. Engineers first create enriched BIM models, then validate these models for completeness of device definitions. The validated models are fed to a transformation engine that automatically derives structured semantic representations and produces deployable DT artefacts with minimal manual coding. The runtime environment exposes standardized extension points that enable DT Engineers to plug in custom services, analytics or control logic, thereby managing DT evolution without redeploying the entire system.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin provides decision support through continuous real‑time data exchange and immersive visualisation. Sensor streams are synchronised with the digital model, allowing Facility Managers to monitor key performance indicators and intervene via the DT interface. The platform supports integration of analytics modules (e.g., predictive models) as custom services, delivering actionable insights such as anomaly detection or energy‑usage forecasts. Recommendations are presented within the visualisation layer, enabling operators to make informed control actions directly on the physical building.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The approach is built on the Building Information Modeling (BIM) standard as the core semantic foundation. All device definitions, control logic and generated artefacts conform to BIM‑based schemas, ensuring compatibility with existing BIM tools. The framework’s modular architecture defines standardized extension points that follow a common interface contract, enabling interoperability of custom services and analytics across different implementations.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

The abstract and introduction describe a 'Living Lab' as the target environment for the Digital Twin. Quote: 'We demonstrate the practical application of our approach in a Living Lab case study'.

The extraction accurately lists the main services described in the abstract and introduction, such as 'real-time monitoring', 'simulation', and 'optimization'. It correctly identifies the primary users as Facility Managers, citing the text: 'Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts with the twin'. While specific details like 'forecast equipment degradation' and 'actuates dimmers' are reasonable inferences given the context of 'predictive analytics' and 'lighting systems', they are slightly more specific than the source text, which groups these under general 'predictive analytics' and 'control strategies'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document,' but the text explicitly lists specific software frameworks and engines used to implement the Digital Twin. Quote: 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and leverages Unreal Engine'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the real-time and continuous synchronization aspects mentioned in the text, such as 'real-time data exchange' and 'live synchronization.' However, specific details like 'near-zero latency' and adjustable frequencies via runtime configuration are implied but not explicitly detailed in the provided snippets. The text does mention 'real-time' and 'continuous synchronization,' which supports the core idea.

The text describes the framework as a 'central hub' and a 'centralized model,' which aligns with the description of a single-instance deployment. There is no mention of multiple or hierarchical instances in the provided snippets, so the description is accurate based on the available evidence.

The text provides strong support for the modular, service-oriented architecture and standardized extension points. It also mentions the DT Engine orchestrating bidirectional data exchange and roles like 'Building Control Engineer' and 'Facility Manager,' which matches the description well. The quotes 'modular, service-oriented DT platforms' and 'standardized extension points' are particularly relevant.

The text discusses interoperability with heterogeneous systems and standardized extension points for integrating external services, which aligns with the description. However, specific data exchange protocols are not mentioned, which is consistent with the description's note that protocols are not enumerated. The quote 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems' supports this.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

Supported by quotes: 'Building Control Engineer... Models and configures devices', 'define... actuators... directly within the BIM environment', 'Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and Ditto, enabling live synchronization'.

The description is well-supported by the text. It correctly states sensors are modeled as BIM entities by the Building Control Engineer, capture post-occupancy/indoor environmental data, and stream via real-time exchange. It also accurately notes the absence of specific sampling/accuracy figures. Supported by quotes: 'define sensors... directly within the BIM environment', 'post-occupancy sensor data', 'real-time data exchange', 'continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided source text does not contain information regarding model fidelity requirements, uncertainty sources, error detection, or validation and verification methods as defined in the characteristic description. While it mentions an 'entanglement metric' for evaluation, this does not align with the requested definitions of fidelity, validity, or uncertainty quantification.

The extraction correctly highlights the reliance on BIM as the semantic foundation and the use of standardized extension points. The text explicitly mentions standardizing BIM semantics and ensuring compatibility with AECO standards.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

While it mentions that MDE 'strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies... promoting compliance,' it does not provide specific details regarding data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, consent management, or data anonymization for the system.

The source text describes the integration of 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange' and general interoperability, but it does not describe specific security and safety measures. There is no mention of cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, or safety-critical system considerations in the provided excerpts.
